sentences.text: That was established in March '43.
And all the Jews--
'41-- excuse me.
Yeah, '43 we have been to [INAUDIBLE] to the camps.
In '41, they came in order, and they gave us I think two weeks or something like this.
All the Jews of Crakow have to move into a closed area, which was-- it was even not the so-called Crakow ghetto from before the war.
And Crakow ghetto from before there war was a place where most of the Jews were living, especially these religious Jews.
It wasn't close at that time, but it was called a ghetto.
But it wasn't the same place.
It was Crakow-- there's a-- the biggest river of Poland is also passing through the Crakow and divides Crakow in two parts.
So we have to go to the other part of the river, which is called Podgorze.
And that was established the ghetto.
In the-- in the very beginning the ghetto was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quite big.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We moved in, then the fences and the stone fence and other fences weren't even ready.
So they were still building them or putting them and it depends, but mostly everybody got one-room apart-- a one-room apartment.
There were no one-room apartments, so we have to-- to be with another family or two families, depends.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Jewish community was-- was-- yeah.
Jewish community was giving the apartments to the-- to the people.
After a short time, the ghetto was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they made it-- they-- they-- closed, I think, a set part of it and threw out all the Jews from there and packed them together.
Jewish-- like-- like Jewish Federation here.
This was the Jewish leadership, established by the-- by the Germans.
It means these were Jewish leaders which were accepted or established by the Germans.
It was called the Jewish community.
So it was called.
Yeah.
Yeah.
There was a-- an office in [INAUDIBLE] which was responsible for it, and then we got-- there we got the address you have to go here and here, this family.
It was-- our family were four people.
So we got a room.
And in the beginning, I think we had even our own kitchen.
And then when the ghetto got smaller, we had to move, because our-- this part where we had been living at the very beginning was taken away from the ghetto.
Maybe they made it also because it was geographically more convenient for them to close, to build the fences and so on.
So we got another apartment, and then we had to squeeze.
And we took parents of my mother or they were taken together with us.
You know, the-- and there were another two children from the family living with us.
And in this way, we could get an apartment for us.
That means not having the kitchen with somebody else.
And we stayed there-- here in this place until the very end.
That means until the very end of the ghetto.
Only belongings that you to take on the-- with you.
That means that you could schlep.
So you could schlep a wagon.
So you may imagine.
No-- all-- almost no furnitures.
So furnitures were left by somebody, because the Poles were living there.
And they felt the Poles from there.
So some furnitures were left.
Some we brought.

You know, you-- in the very beginning, the first two, three, four weeks, there was-- it was-- the procedure was such a one that if somebody was able to do it, they could bring a little bit more things if you had the apartment in order to put them.
So everybody had something but not too much.
And you didn't need, because if you got one bedroom, and you were used to four, so you didn't need too many things.
But otherwise, you take whatever you can schlep.
So everybody-- there were-- the Poles were selling wagons to Jews, and Jews were schlepping the wagons.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The men of the family were schlepping them and bringing some goods.
This wasn't the problem, because at that time-- you must understand that until that time, no-- the Jews hadn't had no furs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were taken away.
And all silver and all-- everything that was precious was taken away.
So there was-- nobody was fighting to take more things.
The-- the understanding of values changed with the times.
So I don't remember that we had much trouble with what we take or what we don't take or it's a pity that we cannot take.
This was the easiest problem to solve.
In the very-- now I have to go back.
Already being here-- not in ghetto.
Everybody had to work somewhere.
And the real working places was-- were with Germans, because otherwise you weren't sure.
It was already a long time that we were thrown out of our business.
And all the Jews stopped working or having their businesses, even with these trustees or trusted men.
So the people tried to get shops in with the Germans.
So all of us were somewhere working.

So when we came to the ghetto, we were going out to ghetto every morning in groups, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most of the places where the Jews were working with Germans were big groups, 100, 500, even 1,000 people.
It depend.
It was with the army.
It was with some organizations and so on.
So we're going out every morning at 5:00 in the morning and coming back organized.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And coming back at 6:00 or 5:00, 6:00 in the evening.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We weren't allowed to go everybody to-- his way.
We're going all together, patrolled by the Germans, by the SS, and so-- so this was the way of living.
Everybody who came back was so tired.
There was almost no-- nothing more to do in the evenings.
We young people were-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I see-- I think were-- were coming together more than the-- it was mostly so that people were coming together.
People were living in two, three houses around them.

Because there was mostly it was a curfew or people were afraid to go out in the night, because the Germans were drunk or so on-- so the patrolling Germans-- so they could shoot everybody if they made it, because they were drunk or just for fun.
And we the youngsters, we did gather together more but not too much.
Then about the food, we got our rations-- food rations.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We bought them.
We bought them.
There were places where you could buy, but everything was rationed.
People who were going out have two possibilities.
The one possibility, which was used by all of us that we ate, we were able to by the Poles foods and to eat them outside.
That means eating by the work.
Then you came at home not hungry.
The second possibility was, which was use especially by people who wanted to earn money, is to smuggle food for the others.
If they succeeded, it was very good.

If they didn't succeed, so there were many, many, many cases when not only people were caught smuggling food were punished, but every set, every 10, all the group and so, there were all even cases when they shot the people who were smuggling food.
This was the way how the-- but as long as ghetto existed-- I am speaking about the Crakow ghetto-- there was still-- many people were in touch with Polish people, all the working places.
Oh, they were coming through the fences, you know.
They-- they knew somebody was selling and buying.
It wasn't-- it was very dangerous, but-- but it was.

So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war when I-- even during the war-- when I knew what was going on, let's say in Warsaw ghetto, where the majority was hunger and very, very poor, so Crakow ghetto, there was hunger, and there were poor people, but I think that on the-- on general-- in general, we managed.
Where did you work?
I was working when-- today I would call it garage.
It was a very special garage, which belonged to a Jew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the war, then it was taken by the Germans.
But the Jew still managed it.
He was a very-- he was a very well-known mechanic.
It was a very special garage which was for cars.
You know, at that time, the electrical-- how do you call-- generator?
Where-- where it wasn't like now-- it was-- you have to have a car, not only an engine but a car, and this was next to a generator.
And the car gave the power to generator, not the-- there was no special machine like now it's all together.
It was a car.
So we were a garage which was specialist for repairing this so-called generator cars.
So it was called [INAUDIBLE] from German.
So it was quite a good working place, because we were on few, only few.
There were seven or eight Jews working there.
And the whole garage maybe 20 people, 25 people.
And the Germans needed these people.
Me and another two colleagues, we have been so-called mechanics, but with the time we learned.
But the others were real professional people before the war.
So they needed us very much.
At that time, generators were very critical.
And maybe they were also rare.
I don't know.
So we were kept quite-- but, again, we were belonging officially to a big group from another place.
So we have been brought every day-- every day.
We have been taken out of the ghetto with a group of maybe 500 Jews-- excuse me-- and then we, eight of us or seven of us-- with time, only five-- we were taken by a special car to this garage.
And then on the way back, we met the group in some special place where.
We were waiting for them and going back to the-- How did you get to work in this garage?
How were you chosen?
I really-- not chosen.
Everybody had to look after their work.
I really don't remember.
I think through a friend.
I think through a friend.
I-- I-- witness every-- every-- you know, this-- you call it a gathering.
I don't know how to call it, because it's-- it was called-- there was a special word found by Germans, which is called akcia.
But you cannot now say it is an action, because it was-- it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there is no-- not a word that you can translate it.
It was something that they-- they decided that on this day, on these two days, there will be an-- let's call it for the--
--for this purpose akcia or action.
That means that all Jews of the ghetto have to gather on-- in a special place, which was a big place.
And we lived very close to this.
So if I wanted another, so it was-- and their decision was every time-- we knew it at the time-- this akcia has to bring, let's say 5,000 or 10,000 Jews to be sent away.
So they gathered the people.
The goal was 5,000-- let's say 5,000 people.
So they gathered them, and they took-- the purpose was to take away the elderly and the sick or the very young or somebody who doesn't work, has no-- no identity card that he works.
This was the-- the goal.
It was announced, everybody who doesn't work, everybody who is sick, everybody who is over let's say 50 or 60 or 70 or something.
But they didn't pay too-- too much attention to it.
The goal was to fulfill the 5,000, let's say, on this day.
So in that moment, they had 5,000, it didn't interest them if they really have only the sick and only the elderly, only the young, only the people who didn't work.
And if they haven't enough, so it didn't interest them to look after this who didn't work, who was-- who was in the region, who they can-- could catch at that very moment, they took them.
In the moment the 5,000 were-- were fulfilled, they went away.
They-- and the others were free.
So it-- it was-- once in a while, we had two or three very, very big so-called akcia, where more-- thousands and thousands were sent away.
And most of the Crakow Jews were deported.
At that time, most of the Crakow Jews went to [INAUDIBLE], and they were all killed.
Nobody survived.
From this transport, nobody survives-- survived-- but very, very few who jumped from the trains.
It was quite a way from Crakow.
So there were very few that jumped and succeeded to-- not to be shot and to come back or to say the story-- to tell the story.
Was anybody from your family part of this akcia?
I should say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] only my-- my parents and my sister.
We were left.
All the family went with-- with one of these akcia.
That means sisters-- sisters of my father, their children, parents of my mother, father of my father.
All the-- all the family were left-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were left only with three cousins.
When the ghetto was-- was liquidated, and we went to the camp, we were left only my parents, my sister, and me, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my three cousins.
I think this is all-- maybe some other-- someone else, but the whole family went with this.
As I told you, the-- the majority of Crakow Jews went by-- at the end, the ghetto was a very small one.
That means a quantity of the people was-- I don't know how many, but we were only a part of the-- before the war, there were 35,000 Jews in Crakow.

During the beginning-- after the beginning of the war or after the beginning of the occupation, with the beginning of the persecution of the Jews, let's say in '39, beginning of '40-- or during '40, they brought very many, many, many Jews from the villages around Crakow and the small places around Crakow.
So part of them, they were-- they were killed and then-- but the other part, the people who were so-called strong enough to work, were brought to Crakow.
So we are getting more than we were before the war.
But at the time, we were less and less and less and less.
I don't remember the-- how many we were, but I really don't know.
Can you tell us what happened during the liquidation of the ghetto?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At least a month, I think, before the liquidation-- maybe two months-- they divided the ghetto into an A and B.

And then we felt that something has-- has to-- to become with us, and something has to occur, because all the people who were working were put in ghetto A, and all that elderly and not working and very, very young and sick and the hospital-- and so hospitalized people and so on were put in ghetto B.

And you have to know, we were living in such a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not knowing what will happen that there were even people who could work or who had to work, so-called a very good place to work, and they stopped working because they-- their-- they developed a kind of philosophy it's better not to work, not to be involved, not to be mixed with the German, but to stay what is called now ghetto B.
Then I can hide myself or so on and so on.
Even such cases were there.
So we were divided in these two ghettos, and then came a day-- in the meantime, we knew they are beginning to build a camp not far from Crakow, outside in Crakow.
It's very close to Crakow.
Now it belongs to Crakow, this place.
It was the place where the Jewish cemetery was before.
So they took out the stones and began to build [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the camp, which is Plaszow.
How did you know about it?
First of all, it was a very close.
Secondly, as I told you, many of us were going out.
So you could hear from both.
You could contact.

So there was one day when the ghetto was closed hermetically, and the Germans were selecting-- selecting people to go out but not to go out to work, because during a normal akcia, they allowed, let's say, our group to go out to work.
And then the others stayed.
This, who was allowed, went out to Plaszow-- was sent to the camp.
And the-- and the other were left.
Who was left was the whole ghetto B and a part of ghetto A.
And all these were killed afterwards.
And this is the beginnings-- this is March '43, which I mentioned before, and I-- it was a mistake.
In March '43, the Crakow ghetto was liquidated, and we-- oh, the Crakow Jews, let's say, went to Plaszow, which was in the first stage so-called working camp.
My-- my father, my mother was with me.
And in the beginning, my sister was in a place-- she was working in a place where they were also overnight there.
She came to Plaszow a few months later.
She was working at the airfield-- Crakow airfield.
Not far from Crakow was an airfield, which was there for the German Air Force airfield.
And the people who worked there didn't come back.
So they were sleeping there, and they have even not bad conditions there.
But they-- it was liquidated a few months after we were in Plaszow.
So she-- she joined us later, if you can call it joining.
